{"content": "     1|# Day 2 Part 1 — Stocks & Options Masterclass
     2|**YouTube:** https://youtu.be/slZ2Uj2tKM0  
     3|**Duration:** 1h 43m 7s  
     4|**Teacher:** Victor
     5|
     6|---
     7|
     8|## Why Options (Not Just Stocks)?
     9|- Stocks = capital appreciation only
    10|- Dividends alone won't generate enough cash flow
    11|- **Options = cash flow machine** alongside your stock portfolio
    12|- Warren Buffett himself trades options — his favourite strategy: **Selling Put Options**
    13|- His quote: "Derivatives are financial weapons of mass destruction" → because most people use them to SPECULATE, not properly
    14|- True purpose of options: **reduce risk + amplify returns**
    15|- Options amplify whatever your stocks do → must pick HIGH QUALITY businesses first
    16|- Selling options on speculative stocks = amplify your losses
    17|
    18|---
    19|
    20|## The #1 Rule Before Selling Options
    21|> Rate each stock 1–10. Only sell options on **9s and 10s**.
    22|> If the stock market closed for 10 years, are you comfortable holding this? If yes → right company. If uncertain → big NO.
    23|
    24|---
    25|
    26|## Options Basics
    27|
    28|### Two Types of Options
    29|| Option Type | You Are... | You Make Money When... |
    30||-------------|-----------|----------------------|
    31|| Buy Call | Bullish | Share price goes UP |
    32|| Buy Put | Bearish | Share price goes DOWN |
    33|| **Sell Call** | Bearish | Share price stays flat/down |
    34|| **Sell Put** | Bullish | Share price stays flat/up |
    35|
    36|**Memory trick:** Phone on table → Call comes in → you PICK UP (Call = up). Done with call → you PUT DOWN your phone (Put = down).
    37|
    38|### 3 Terms in Every Options Contract
    39|1. **What stock** — which company you promise to buy/sell
    40|2. **Strike price** — at what price you promise to buy/sell
    41|3. **Expiry date** — how long the contract lasts (1 week, 1 month, 1 year, etc.)
    42|You decide ALL THREE.
    43|
    44|### At Expiry: Only 2 Things Can Happen
    45|- **A) Expire Worthless** — contract becomes valueless to buyer → **SELLER WINS** (like insurance company collecting premium, nothing bad happened)
    46|- **B) Gets Exercised** — something bad happened, contract triggers → buyer claims
    47|
    48|**Goal as option seller: Make outcome A happen more than B** → only sell on high quality businesses
    49|
    50|---
    51|
    52|## Selling Options = Acting Like an Insurance Company
    53|- You sell a **promise** (contract) and collect **premium** upfront
    54|- Premium = your cash flow

    74|- ~2 corrections (>10% drop) per year on average

    87|## During Down Cycles — How to Analyse

   100|## The Two Golden Rules of Selling Options
   101|1. **Promise what you WANT** — you must truly want to own (sell put) or truly want to sell (sell call) the shares

   111|## Key Terminologies for Option Selling

   174|

   178|- Premium received = per share amount × 100

   182|| Scenario | What Happens | You Win/Lose? |
   183||----------|-------------|---------------|
   184|| Stock goes UP a lot | Expires worthless | ✅ Keep premium |
   185|| Stock goes UP a little | Expires worthless | ✅ Keep premium |
   186|| Stock stays FLAT | Expires worthless | ✅ Keep premium |
   187|| Stock drops a LITTLE (above strike) | Expires worthless | ✅ Keep premium |
   188|| Stock drops BELOW strike | Exercised — you buy shares | ⚠️ Temporary paper loss (but you own quality stock) |
   189|
   190|**You only truly lose if:**
   191|1. You sell on a speculative/bad company
   192|2. The drop is permanent (company dies)
   193|
   194|For high-quality companies → temporary paper loss → eventual recovery = you win long term
   195|

   197|
   198|## Worked Examples
   199|
   200|### Example 1: Tesla (High IV)
   201|- Market price: $185
   202|- Strike price: $180 (OTM — $5 discount)
   203|- Contract: 1 month, 1 contract (100 shares)
   204|- Premium per share: $11.05

   214|- Total premium: **$250**
   215|- Cash needed: $95 × 100 = **$9,500**
   216|- ROI: $2.50 ÷ $95 = **2.63% per month** ← even low IV still beats most investments
   217|
   218|### Warren Buffett's Coca-Cola Trade
   219|- Coke trading at $39, Buffett wanted to buy at $35
   220|- Instead of placing a limit order → he **sold 50,000 put contracts** at $35 strike
   221|- Premium: $1.50/share × 5,000,000 shares = **$7.5 million collected upfront**
   222|- If price stays above $35 → keeps $7.5M, never buys
   223|- If price drops to $35 → buys at $35 as intended AND keeps $7.5M premium
   224|- Lesson: **Get paid while waiting to buy the stock you wanted anyway**

   227|
   228|## Capital Allocation for Selling Options
   229|- Minimum portfolio to start selling options: **USD 25,000**
   230|- Below 25K → focus on **capital appreciation only** (more patience required)
   231|- For larger portfolios: own 200 shares of same stock → 100 for capital appreciation, 100 for selling covered calls
   232|- Selling options = **fixed, capped cashflow** (e.g. 3%/month is the max)
   233|- Stock ownership = **unlimited upside**

   238|## Execution Checklist — Sell Put Strategy

   172|- Asking price: $100,000
   173|- P/E = 10 → you pay 10 years' worth of profits
   174|
   175|| P/E Range | Interpretation |
   176||-----------|----------------|
   177|| 8–12 | "Cheap" — market expects slow growth (Warren Buffett's preferred range) |
   178|| 30–50 | "Expensive" — market expects fast future growth |
   179|| 60+ | Very speculative (e.g. Tesla) |
   180|
   181|**How to use it properly:**
   182|- Don't compare to old P/E benchmarks — **compare current vs 5-year average**
   183|- Compare within same industry (tech P/E always higher than consumer staples)
   184|- Apple: current 32.9 vs 5yr avg 30 → slightly overvalued but not crazy
   185|- Coca-Cola: current 25.6 vs 5yr avg 26.7 → slightly undervalued
   186|
   187|**Limitation:** Old-fashioned metric (Buffett's era). High-quality companies often stay "overvalued" by P/E standards for years. Don't wait years for it to become "cheap" — just buy on red days.
   188|
   189|**Most useful for:** Large, stable, profitable companies (blue chips)
   190|
   191|---
   192|
   193|### Method 2: P/B Ratio (Price to Book)
   194|**Formula:** Stock Price ÷ Book Value Per Share
   195|
   196|**What it means:** How much you pay vs company's actual physical net assets
   197|
   198|**Example:**
   199|- Car worth $50k in parts (book value)
   200|- You pay $100k → P/B = 2.0
   201|- Paying 2x because you believe the car/brand has extra value
   202|
   203|| P/B | Interpretation |
   204||-----|----------------|
   205|| > 1 | Paying more than asset value — believing in future growth |
   206|| < 1 | Buying cheaper than asset value — could be opportunity OR danger |
   207|
   208|**Most useful for:** Asset-heavy companies — banks, insurance, real estate, manufacturers
   209|**NOT useful for:** Tech companies (their value is IP, software, brand — not physical assets)
   210|
   211|**Example:**
   212|- Goldman Sachs: 5yr avg P/B = 1.38, current = 2.37 → **EXPENSIVE**
   213|- Berkshire Hathaway: current ≈ fair value
   214|
   215|---
   216|
   217|### Method 3: P/S Ratio (Price to Sales) ← Victor's Preferred
   218|**Formula:** Market Cap ÷ Annual Revenue (Sales)
   219|
   220|**What it means:** How much investors pay for every $1 of the company's sales/revenue
   221|
   222|**Why useful:** Works even for companies not yet profitable (no earnings = can't use P/E)
   223|
   224|**Most useful for:**
   225|- Fast-growing tech startups
   226|- SaaS, AI, cloud companies
   227|- Companies reinvesting heavily (Shopify, Zoom in early days)
   228|- High-growth with losses → P/E useless, use P/S
   229|
   230|**Examples:**
   231|- Nvidia during AI boom: P/S ~20 (investors paid $20 per $1 of sales)
   232|- Ford/traditional auto: P/S 0.3–0.5 (slow growth industries)
   233|- Crowdstrike: current P/S ≈ 22 vs 5yr avg 23 → **fair value**
   234|- Unity: current P/S ≈ 4.84 vs 5yr avg 7 → **undervalued** (but dig deeper why)
   235|
   236|**Victor's rule:**
   237|- Company profitable → use **PEG ratio**
   238|- Company NOT yet profitable → use **P/S ratio**
   239|- Sales harder to manipulate than earnings → more reliable signal
   240|- Low P/S + strong sales growth = potential multi-bagger
   241|
   242|---
   243|
   244|### Method 4: PEG Ratio (Price/Earnings to Growth) — Peter Lynch
   245|**Formula:** P/E Ratio ÷ Earnings Growth Rate
   246|
   247|**What it means:** Is the stock expensive OR cheap relative to how fast the company is growing?
   248|
   249|**The fruit tree analogy:**
   250|- Tree A: costs $100, grows 5 apples/year
   251|- Tree B: costs $150, grows 20 apples/year
   252|- B is actually better value despite higher price
   253|
   254|| PEG Value | Interpretation |
   255||-----------|----------------|
   256|| < 1 | Potential bargain — growing faster than priced |
   257|| ≈ 1 | Fairly valued |
   258|| > 1 | Potentially expensive for growth rate |
   259|
   260|**Example:**
   261|- Tech company: P/E = 40, growth rate = 40% → PEG = 1 → fairly valued
   262|- Slow company: P/E = 15, growth rate = 5% → PEG = 3 → expensive for slow growth
   263|
   264|**Most useful for:** Growing companies with consistent earnings (established tech, consumer brands, healthcare innovators)
   265|**Use on Morningstar.com** to find the PEG ratio
   266|

   270|

   272||-------------|----------|
   273|| Large, stable, profitable (blue chips) | P/E ratio (compare vs 5yr avg) |
   274|| Banks, insurers, real estate, manufacturers | P/B ratio |
   275|| Fast-growing tech, not yet profitable | P/S ratio |
   276|| Profitable growth companies | PEG ratio |
   277|| Tech companies (general) | P/S + PEG |
   278|

   294|
   295|### On Selling Call Options
   296|- Think of it as **squeezing additional dividend** from shares you already hold
   297|- Not the end of the world if stock gets called away — you sell at profit + keep premium
